chunks.content: YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Complete Technical Documentation Audio Intelligence · Real-Time LED Control · Beat Synchronisation Platform ESP32 (Arduino) + Python 3 LED Driver FastLED — WS2812B × 100 Audio AI Google YAMNet (TensorFlow Hub) Transport HTTP/GET over Wi-Fi (LAN) Version 2.0 (post-fixes) This document covers the full system architecture, every HTTP route, all signal-processing decisions, and the complete list of bug-fixes applied. -- 1 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation Contents 1 System Overview 3 1.1 Block Diagram . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3 1.2 Key Design Decisions . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3 2 Python Side: Audio Pipeline 3 2.1 Audio Capture . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 3 2.2 YAMNet Classification . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 4 2.2.1 What is YAMNet? . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 4 2.2.2 How it is used here . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 4 2.2.3 Mood Map . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 4 2.3 RMS and EMA Smoothing . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 5 2.4 Music Holdoff . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 5 2.5 Beat Detection . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 5 2.5.1 Algorithm . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 5 2.6 HTTP Send Logic . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Reduced (10 s → 4 s) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 10 5.6 Summary Table . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 11 6 Configuration Reference 11 6.1 Python Parameters . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 12 6.2 ESP32 Parameters . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 12 7 Sequence Diagrams 12 7.1 Music Mode Entry and RMS Update . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 12 7.2 Speech Override During Music . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 13 8 Wiring Summary 13 9 Setup & Usage 13 9.1 ESP32 (Arduino) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 14 1 -- 2 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation 9.2 Python Host . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 14 9.3 Tuning Tips . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 14 10 Known Limitations 14 2 -- 3 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation System Overview The YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car is a two-part embedded system that classifies live microphone audio on a host PC and translates that classification into real-time LED animations on a small ESP32-powered car. Block Diagram Stage Description Hardware/Software 1 Microphone captures 10 ms audio chunks at 16 kHz PC microphone 2 YAMNet classifies a 1-second sliding window every 0.5 s Python / TF Hub 3 Per-chunk RMS computed; EMA-smoothed for LED speed Python (NumPy) 4 Beat detector fires on per-chunk RMS tran- sients Python 5 HTTP GET requests sent to ESP32 over Wi-Fi LAN urllib 6 ESP32 web server receives commands and up- dates LEDs Arduino / FastLED 7 Motors run independently of LED state L298N driver Key Design Decisions • Separation of concerns: The Python side handles all AI inference and signal processing. The ESP32 only runs LED patterns and motors — it never does audio analysis.

server receives commands and up- dates LEDs Arduino / FastLED 7 Motors run independently of LED state L298N driver Key Design Decisions • Separation of concerns: The Python side handles all AI inference and signal processing. The ESP32 only runs LED patterns and motors — it never does audio analysis. • Non-blocking everywhere: Both sides avoid delay(). The ESP32 uses a state machine for the LED flush sequence; Python processes each 10 ms audio chunk without sleeping. • Fire-and-forget HTTP: All requests use a short 0.5 s timeout and are not retried. A missed beat or RMS update is invisible to the eye; blocking the audio pipeline on a network error would be far worse. Python Side: Audio Pipeline Audio Capture sounddevice.InputStream opens the default microphone at 16 kHz, mono, float32. The callback deposits each 10 ms chunk (160 samples) into a thread-safe queue.Queue. Listing 1: Mic callback 1 def mic_callback ( indata , frames , time_info , status ) : 2 chunk = indata [: , 0]. copy () 3 try : 4 audio_queue . put_nowait ( chunk ) 3 -- 4 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation 5 except queue . Full : 6 audio_queue . get_nowait () # drop oldest 7 audio_queue . put_nowait ( chunk ) The queue has a hard cap of 200 entries (≈2 seconds). If the main loop falls behind, the oldest chunk is silently dropped rather than blocking the sound-card callback thread. YAMNet Classification What is YAMNet? YAMNet is a deep convolutional neural network trained on AudioSet — a dataset of over 2 million YouTube clips labelled with 521 sound classes. It uses a MobileNetV1 backbone operating on log-mel spectrograms. Given a waveform it returns a matrix of class scores (one row per 0.48 s patch), which are averaged into a single 521-dimensional score vector. How it is used here 1. A sliding window of the last 1.0 s of audio is assembled from the 100-chunk deque. 2. YAMNet is called at most every 0.5 seconds (throttled by YAMNET INTERVAL). Running it on every 10 ms chunk would saturate the CPU and the WS2812B strip would never change in real time. 3. The top-scoring class name is mapped to one of five moods via MOOD MAP keyword matching. 4. A minimum confidence of 0.15 is enforced; any top class below that threshold collapses to “Idle”. Listing 2: Classification + mood mapping 1 def classify ( yamnet , class_names , waveform ) : 2 scores , _ , _ = yamnet ( tf . convert_to_tensor ( waveform , dtype = tf . float32 ) ) 3 mean_scores = tf . reduce_mean ( scores , axis =0) . numpy () 4 top = int ( np . argmax ( mean_scores ) ) 5 conf = float ( mean_scores [ top ]) 6 label = class_names [ top ] 7 return mood_for_class ( label , conf ) , label , conf Mood Map Mood LED Effect Matched YAMNet Classes (key- words)

4 top = int ( np . argmax ( mean_scores ) ) 5 conf = float ( mean_scores [ top ]) 6 label = class_names [ top ] 7 return mood_for_class ( label , conf ) , label , conf Mood Map Mood LED Effect Matched YAMNet Classes (key- words) Music Rainbow wave + beat flash music, song, drum, guitar, piano, beat- boxing . . . Clap Solid full red clap, hands, finger snap, applause Speech Solid full blue speech, talk, conversation, narration . . . Alarm Yellow/red flicker alarm, siren, fire alarm, buzzer, beep . . . Idle Solid white silence, quiet, background noise . . . 4 -- 5 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation RMS and EMA Smoothing Raw RMS is computed per 10 ms chunk: RMSraw = v u u t 1 N N −1 X i=0 x2 i Because a single 160-sample window at 16 kHz is extremely short, raw RMS is very noisy. An Exponential Moving Average (EMA) smooths it: RMSsmooth[n] = α · RMSraw[n] + (1 − α) · RMSsmooth[n − 1] α = 0.15 With α = 0.15 the effective time constant is τ = Tchunk/α = 10 ms/0.15 ≈ 67 ms , which gives perceptibly smooth LED speed changes without noticeable lag. Note: Beat detection still uses raw per-chunk RMS because it needs sensitivity to sharp transients. Only the LED speed and resend-threshold comparisons use smoothed RMS. Music Holdoff YAMNet classifies from a 1-second window and is only called every 0.5 s. Between calls it may return “Idle” during a brief quiet passage (e.g. a drum break), which would erroneously switch the rainbow off. The holdoff solves this: 1. Every time YAMNet returns “Music”, last music classify time is stamped with time.monotonic(). 2. On every loop iteration, if fewer than 4 seconds have elapsed since that stamp and the current mood is “Idle”, the effective mood is overridden to “Music”. 3. Speech and Clap always bypass the holdoff immediately. When either fires, last music classify time is reset to zero, which collapses the holdoff window. Listing 3: Holdoff logic 1 if current_mood in ( " Speech " , " Clap " ) : 2 last_music_classify_time = 0.0 # cancel holdoff 3 in_holdoff = False 4 effective_mood = current_mood 5 elif in_holdoff and current_mood == " Idle " : 6 effective_mood = " Music " 7 else : 8 effective_mood = current_mood Beat Detection Beat detection runs on every 10 ms chunk — it is not throttled because beats can be as close as 250 ms apart at 240 BPM. Algorithm 1. Keep a rolling deque of the last 43 raw-RMS values (43 × 10 ms = 430 ms). 5 -- 6 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation 2. Compute the rolling mean ¯r of that window. 3. A beat fires when: RMSraw > ¯r × 1.5 AND ∆tbeat ≥ 250 ms 4. The BPM is estimated from the gap between the previous two

ms). 5 -- 6 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation 2. Compute the rolling mean ¯r of that window. 3. A beat fires when: RMSraw > ¯r × 1.5 AND ∆tbeat ≥ 250 ms 4. The BPM is estimated from the gap between the previous two beats: BPM = 60 min(∆t, 2.0 s) 5. The URL /led/beat?bpm=${bpm} is sent to the ESP32. The ESP32 uses the BPM to set the beat-flash fade speed (faster music ⇒ shorter fade-back). Beat detection is gated on effective mood == "Music", so it stops automatically when Speech or Clap overrides. No extra guard is needed. HTTP Send Logic Condition Action Mood changed (e.g. Idle → Music) Send /led/rainbow?rms=... (enters rainbow mode once) Already in Music, smoothed RMS shifted ≥ 0.008 Send /led/music rms?rms=... (speed-only update, no reset) Beat detected during Music Send /led/beat?bpm=... Speech detected Cancel holdoff; send /led/blue Clap detected Cancel holdoff; send /led/red blink Alarm detected Send /led/alarm?rms=... Idle (no holdoff) Send /led/idle ESP32 Side: LED Controller Hardware • LED strip: 100 × WS2812B (GRB order), data pin GPIO 5. • Motors: Two DC motors via an L298N bridge, PWM on GPIO 26/27, direction pins 32/33/25/14. Motor code is untouched by LED changes. • Brightness: FastLED.setBrightness(120) — roughly 47% of full brightness for power/heat reasons. 6 -- 7 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation Pattern Enum Listing 4: Pattern states 1 enum LedPattern { 2 PATTERN_IDLE , // Solid white -- one - shot 3 PATTERN_SPEECH , // Solid blue -- one - shot 4 PATTERN_CLAP , // Solid red -- one - shot 5 PATTERN_MUSIC , // Rainbow wave + beat flash 6 PATTERN_ALARM , // Yellow / red flicker 7 }; currentPattern is read every loop() tick by updateLeds(), which dispatches to the appropriate animation branch. Non-Blocking Flush State Machine WS2812B LEDs require a > 50 μs low signal to latch. When switching from a fast-changing rainbow pattern to a solid colour, residual charge in the signal line can cause partial latching (only the first ∼30 LEDs update). The flush state machine solves this by sending three blank (black) frames with 25 ms gaps before painting the target colour, without ever calling delay(). State Time Action FL BLK1 immediately Fill all LEDs black, show() FL GAP1 wait 25 ms — FL BLK2 after gap Fill black, show() FL GAP2 wait 25 ms — FL BLK3 after gap Fill black, show() FL GAP3 wait 25 ms — FL PAINT after gap Fill target colour, show(), set patternInitDone=true This path is used for Idle (white), and originally for Speech/Clap. See Section 3.4 for why Speech/Clap bypass it. Immediate Hard-Paint (paintImmediate) The flush state machine takes ≈75 ms to complete. During that window the PATTERN MUSIC branch in updateLeds() continues scrolling the rainbow and overwrites the LEDs before the final paint step. This was the root cause of Speech and Clap failing to override rainbow. paintImmediate() solves it by: 1. Setting flushState = FL IDLE

takes ≈75 ms to complete. During that window the PATTERN MUSIC branch in updateLeds() continues scrolling the rainbow and overwrites the LEDs before the final paint step. This was the root cause of Speech and Clap failing to override rainbow. paintImmediate() solves it by: 1. Setting flushState = FL IDLE so runFlush() exits immediately on the next call. 2. Setting beatActive = false and beatFadeStep = 0 so the beat-flash branch also exits. 3. Writing every LED, calling FastLED.show() three times with 300 μs gaps (enough for a clean latch on all 100 LEDs). 4. Setting patternInitDone = true so updateLeds() will not attempt a second flush on the next tick. 7 -- 8 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation Listing 5: paintImmediate – used by /led/blue and /led/red blink 1 static void paintImmediate ( CRGB colour ) { 2 flushState = FL_IDLE ; 3 beatActive = false ; 4 beatFadeStep = 0; 5 for ( int i = 0; i < NUM_LEDS ; i ++) leds [ i ] = colour ; 6 FastLED . show () ; 7 delayMicroseconds (300) ; 8 FastLED . show () ; 9 delayMicroseconds (300) ; 10 FastLED . show () ; 11 patternInitDone = true ; 12 } Why three show() calls? Each WS2812B needs the data line to be held low for ≥ 50 μs to latch. One call is usually enough in isolation, but when transitioning from an active rainbow the LED driver IC may be mid-cycle. Three calls with 300 μs gaps guarantee a clean reset and a solid latch for all 100 LEDs. Music Pattern: Rainbow + Beat Flash Rainbow Scroll On each tick, if now - lastLedUpdate >= waveInterval: 1. waveOffset++ (uint8 wraps cleanly at 256). 2. Each LED i gets hue = waveOffset + (i × 256)/100. 3. FastLED.show() is called. waveInterval is updated in real time by /led/music rms: waveInterval = 200 − 192 · s min RMS 0.35 , 1  ms/step At RMS = 0 the strip scrolls at 200 ms/step (slow, ambient). At RMS ≥ 0.35 it scrolls at 8 ms/step (very fast, energetic). Beat Flash When /led/beat is received while currentPattern == PATTERN MUSIC: 1. beatActive = true, beatFadeStep = 0. 2. beatFadeInterval is computed from the received BPM: beatFadeInterval = 35 − 27 · BPM − 40 200 ms/frame At 40 BPM: 35 ms/frame. At 240 BPM: 8 ms/frame. 3. Frame 0: fill all LEDs white (the flash). 4. Frames 1–8: blend from white back to rainbow using leds[i] = blend(White, rainbowi, t255) t255 = ⌊step × 255/8⌋ 8 -- 9 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation 5. After step 8, beatActive = false and normal scroll resumes. Alarm Pattern Yellow (0xFF, 0xC8, 0x00) and red (0xFF, 0x00, 0x00) alternate every alarmInterval ms, where: alarmInterval = 120 − 90 · s min RMS 0.35 , 1  ms Loud alarm sounds produce rapid flickering; quiet ones produce slow alternation. HTTP API Reference

= false and normal scroll resumes. Alarm Pattern Yellow (0xFF, 0xC8, 0x00) and red (0xFF, 0x00, 0x00) alternate every alarmInterval ms, where: alarmInterval = 120 − 90 · s min RMS 0.35 , 1  ms Loud alarm sounds produce rapid flickering; quiet ones produce slow alternation. HTTP API Reference All routes are HTTP GET on port 80 of the ESP32’s LAN IP. Python uses a 0.5 s timeout; all requests are fire-and-forget. Route Effect Params /led/idle Non-blocking flush to solid white (Idle / silence). — /led/off Alias for /led/idle. — /led/blue Immediate hard-paint to solid blue. Cancels beat, aborts any pending flush. — /led/red blink Immediate hard-paint to solid red. Cancels beat, aborts any pending flush. — /led/rainbow Enter music mode; sets waveInterval from RMS. Resets animation. Send once on mode entry. rms /led/music rms Update waveInterval only. Animation continues uninterrupted. Send while already in rainbow mode. rms /led/beat Trigger white flash + fade-back. No-op if not in Music. bpm /led/alarm Yellow/red flicker; sets rate from RMS. rms /stop Stop motors. — /go Drive motors forward. — Bug Fixes Applied (v1 → v2) Fix 1 — LED Index Type (uint8 t → int) All LED loops previously used uint8 t i. While this wraps correctly at 256 for 100 iterations, it interacts badly with certain compiler optimisations on Xtensa (ESP32) and with FastLED’s internal DMA buffer indexing when mixed with fill solid calls. Every LED loop is now int i or uint16 t i. Fix 2 — Music Animation Reset on Every RMS Update Problem: Python called /led/rainbow?rms=X every time the smoothed RMS changed. Each call invoked applyPattern(PATTERN MUSIC), which reset waveOffset, beatActive, 9 -- 10 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation and patternInitDone — causing a visible stutter every few hundred milliseconds. Fix: A new route /led/music rms?rms=X only updates waveInterval. Python sends /led/rainbow exactly once on mode entry, then sends /led/music rms for all subsequent RMS updates. Fix 3 — Speech / Clap Cannot Override Rainbow Problem: When Speech or Clap was detected while the rainbow was running, Python sent /led/blue or /led/red blink. The ESP32 handler called applyPattern(), which set patternInitDone = false and started startFlush(). However, the very next loop() tick entered the PATTERN MUSIC branch (the new pattern had not updated currentPattern before loop() ran again), scrolled the rainbow, and called FastLED.show() — overwriting the flush black frame before it could latch. The strip stayed rainbow. Fix (ESP32): /led/blue and /led/red blink now call paintImmediate() which (a) aborts the flush state machine, (b) clears beatActive, and (c) calls FastLED.show() three times before returning. patternInitDone is set to true immediately so the next updateLeds() tick does nothing. Fix (Python): When current mood is Speech or Clap, the code skips the /led/music rms shortcut path entirely and always reaches the route changed block, guaranteeing the HTTP call is made on the very next classified chunk. Fix 4 — Music Holdoff Blocking Speech / Clap Problem: The holdoff was implemented as: 1 if

mood is Speech or Clap, the code skips the /led/music rms shortcut path entirely and always reaches the route changed block, guaranteeing the HTTP call is made on the very next classified chunk. Fix 4 — Music Holdoff Blocking Speech / Clap Problem: The holdoff was implemented as: 1 if in_holdoff and current_mood in ( " Idle " ,) : 2 effective_mood = " Music " 3 else : 4 effective_mood = current_mood This correctly passed Speech and Clap through. However, Python also reset last music classify time only when YAMNet returned Music. After Speech fired, the holdoff window (t < 10 s ) was still active, so on the very next chunk — if YAMNet had not yet re-run — the fallback “else” path picked up current mood = "Music" (last confirmed) and switched back to rainbow within 500 ms. Fix: When Speech or Clap is confirmed, last music classify time is reset to 0.0. This makes time since music very large, collapsing in holdoff to False for all subsequent chunks until the next Music classification. Fix 5 — Holdoff Duration Reduced (10 s → 4 s) 10 seconds caused the rainbow to linger far too long after music ended. 4 seconds covers typical YAMNet instability during brief pauses without annoying the user. 10 -- 11 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation Summary Table # Symptom (v1) Root Cause & Fix (v2) 1 Only first 30 LEDs updated in some patterns uint8 t loop index + mixed fill solid/loop calls; changed all to int 2 Rainbow stutters every few hundred ms Every RMS change called applyPattern(); added /led/music rms for speed-only updates 3 Speech/Clap cannot override rainbow Flush SM too slow; rainbow loop overwrote flush frames; added paintImmediate() + Python short- cut bypass 4 Music re-asserts within 500 ms after Speech/Clap Holdoff clock not reset on override; now zeroed when Speech/Clap fires 5 Rainbow lingers 10 s after music stops Holdoff too long; reduced to 4 s Configuration Reference 11 -- 12 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation Python Parameters Constant Default Effect ESP32 IP 192.168.137.80 ESP32 LAN address SAMPLE RATE 16000 Hz Must match YAMNet requirement CHUNK SECONDS 0.010 s Audio chunk size; 10 ms = 160 sam- ples YAMNET INTERVAL 0.5 s How often YAMNet classifies YAMNET WINDOW 1.0 s Audio fed to YAMNet MIN CONFIDENCE 0.15 Below this, result collapses to Idle HTTP TIMEOUT 0.5 s Per-request network timeout RMS EMA ALPHA 0.15 EMA smoothing factor (α) RMS CHANGE THRESHOLD 0.008 Min smoothed-RMS shift before re- send MUSIC HOLDOFF SECONDS 4.0 s Holdoff window vs Idle BEAT HISTORY 43 chunks Rolling window for beat detection BEAT MULTIPLIER 1.5 Chunk RMS must exceed mean × this BEAT MIN INTERVAL 0.25 s Minimum gap between beats (≤ 240 BPM) BEAT MAX INTERVAL 2.0 s Gap larger than this resets BPM es- timate ESP32 Parameters Constant Default Effect NUM LEDS 100 LED count; change here only LED PIN GPIO 5 WS2812B

Chunk RMS must exceed mean × this BEAT MIN INTERVAL 0.25 s Minimum gap between beats (≤ 240 BPM) BEAT MAX INTERVAL 2.0 s Gap larger than this resets BPM es- timate ESP32 Parameters Constant Default Effect NUM LEDS 100 LED count; change here only LED PIN GPIO 5 WS2812B data pin MOTOR SPEED 180/255 PWM duty for forward drive BEAT FADE STEPS 8 Frames for beat-to-rainbow fade RMS CEIL (wave) 0.35 RMS at which scroll is fastest (8 ms) RMS CEIL (alarm) 0.35 RMS at which alarm is fastest (30 ms) Sequence Diagrams Music Mode Entry and RMS Update Python ESP32 | | |-- classify() -> "Music" --------> | |-- /led/rainbow?rms=0.12 --------> | applyPattern(MUSIC), waveInterval=130ms | | [rainbow scrolling...] 12 -- 13 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation |-- (RMS shifts to 0.22) ---------- | |-- /led/music_rms?rms=0.22 ------> | waveInterval=55ms only (no reset) | | [rainbow faster, no stutter] |-- /led/beat?bpm=124.0 ----------> | beatActive=true, flash white, fade back | | Speech Override During Music Python ESP32 | | | [in_holdoff=True, Music mode] | [rainbow scrolling] |-- classify() -> "Speech" -------> | | last_music_classify_time = 0 | | in_holdoff = False | | effective_mood = "Speech" | |-- /led/blue --------------------> | paintImmediate(blue): | | flushState = FL_IDLE | | beatActive = false | | fill all LEDs blue | | show() x3 | | [strip = solid blue instantly] | last_route = "/led/blue" | | [beat detection stops] | | | Wiring Summary Signal ESP32 GPIO Connected to Notes LED Data GPIO 5 WS2812B DIN 300–500 Ω series resistor recommended Motor PWM A GPIO 26 L298N ENA 1 kHz PWM Motor PWM B GPIO 27 L298N ENB 1 kHz PWM M1A GPIO 32 L298N IN1 M1B GPIO 33 L298N IN2 M2A GPIO 25 L298N IN3 M2B GPIO 14 L298N IN4 Power: WS2812B LEDs draw up to 60 mA each at full white. 100 LEDs = 6 A max. Use a dedicated 5 V supply for the strip; do not power it from the ESP32 3.3 V pin. The ESP32 and L298N motors should also be on separate regulated supplies (or at minimum a high-capacity LiPo with appropriate regulators) to avoid voltage dips during motor acceleration resetting the MCU. Setup & Usage 13 -- 14 of 15 -- YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car Technical Documentation ESP32 (Arduino) 1. Install FastLED via the Arduino Library Manager. 2. Set WIFI SSID and WIFI PASSWORD in the sketch. 3. Set ESP32 IP to match (check Serial Monitor after boot). 4. Flash via Arduino IDE with ESP32 board package installed. Python Host 1. Install dependencies: pip install sounddevice numpy tensorflow tensorflow - hub 2. Set ESP32 IP in yamnet controller.py. 3. Run: python yamnet controller.py 4. YAMNet weights are downloaded automatically on first run (≈15 MB). 5. Point a microphone at the sound source. The terminal prints detected class, mood, smoothed RMS, and the URL sent on every state change. Tuning Tips • Too many false Idle drops: Increase MUSIC HOLDOFF

Run: python yamnet controller.py 4. YAMNet weights are downloaded automatically on first run (≈15 MB). 5. Point a microphone at the sound source. The terminal prints detected class, mood, smoothed RMS, and the URL sent on every state change. Tuning Tips • Too many false Idle drops: Increase MUSIC HOLDOFF SECONDS (e.g. 6–8 s) or lower MIN CONFIDENCE slightly. • Beat fires too often / on non-beats: Raise BEAT MULTIPLIER (e.g. 1.8–2.0). • Beat not firing: Lower BEAT MULTIPLIER or increase microphone gain. • LED speed change feels abrupt: Lower RMS EMA ALPHA (e.g. 0.08) for more smooth- ing. • LED speed change lags behind music energy: Raise RMS EMA ALPHA (e.g. 0.25). Known Limitations • YAMNet is designed for environmental sound classification, not strict music genre or instrument detection. Edge cases (e.g. very quiet music, reverberant rooms) may occasionally misclassify. • HTTP over LAN has ∼1–5 ms latency on a local network but can spike to 50–200 ms on congested Wi-Fi. Beat flash timing is best-effort; the strip may flash slightly off the beat on busy networks. • The ESP32 web server handles one request at a time. If Python sends /led/beat and /led/music rms in rapid succession, the second request queues. The 0.5 s timeout prevents pile-up. • At FastLED brightness 120/255 and 100 LEDs, white (all channels max) draws ≈2.8 A from the 5 V rail. Ensure your PSU is rated for at least 4 A to allow headroom. YAMNet ESP32 LED Master Car — Technical Documentation v2.0 14 -- 15 of 15 --
